description: 'wimura' is the amount or condition of water vapour in air. A room, forest, or weather record may have high or low humidity even when no surface is wet. 'wirua' describes wet material or moisture-laden air as a felt quality; 'wimura' selects the atmospheric parameter for comparison or monitoring.
articulatory_notes: Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then glide into high-front 'i'. Close the lips for 'm' and carry voicing through the nose into stressed high-back rounded 'u'. Make a tap or short trill with the tongue tip and open into central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: Rounded 'wi' and stressed 'mu' keep the mouth gathered before final 'ra' opens broadly. The small-to-wide movement can recall moisture dispersing through air, while the opening also gives a distant echo of 'wirua' (wet).
usage_notes: A source record identifies the technical measure behind 'wimura': relative humidity, absolute humidity, or dew point. It also carries the value and method. The Phi noun selects the atmospheric parameter being compared or monitored.

pillars.solarpunk-values: Humidity links weather and habitat to the design of buildings and cultivation. Energy use and comfort become separate consequences in that account.

semantic_domains.nature: water vapour considered as a condition of weather, climate, or habitat
semantic_domains.physical: an atmospheric parameter whose exact measure depends on a stated method and reference
